figures.caption: Table 1. Frame distribution of social behaviors across all nine RatSI observation videos. The last row indicates the overall percentage of each behavior across all videos (total 202,550 frames).

Figure 4. Overview of the proposed MSGL-Transformer architecture. The model combines global token generation, behavior-aware modulation, and multi-scale attention within a lightweight transformer encoder to jointly capture global context and fine-grained temporal dynamics in rodent social interactions.
Figure 7. ROC curves across the three representative splits of the RatSI dataset.
Table 15. Comparison with published keypoint-based methods on CalMS21 Task 1. Results for methods marked with † are reported from26.
Table 2. Summary of the selected RatSI behaviors that are considered to represent the core range of rodent social interactions.

Figure 2. Sample frames from the RatSI dataset showing the five social behaviors studied in this work: Solitary, Approaching, Following, Moving Away, and Social Nose Contact, spanning from no social engagement to direct physical contact.
Table 8. Cross-validation results of the MSGL-Transformer on the RatSI dataset.

Figure 6. Accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score across three representative splits in RatSI: Valid-7–Test-3 (best-performing), Valid-2–Test-8 (near the mean), and Valid-3–Test-9 (most challenging).

Figure 1. Four consecutive frames for each of the five behaviors in the RatSI18 dataset, showing how each behavior develops over time. Behavior instance durations vary across videos, with Solitary and Following typically spanning longer periods and Moving Away occurring over shorter spans.
Figure 9. ROC curves of the MSGL-Transformer on the CalMS21 test set, showing per-class discrimination performance.
Table 6. Summary of model and training hyperparameters used in the proposed MSGL-Transformer.

Figure 3. Representative video frames from the CalMS21 dataset illustrating the four annotated behavior classes. The resident (black) mouse initiates all active behaviors toward the intruder (white) mouse in the resident-intruder assay24.

Figure 10. Confusion matrix of the MSGL-Transformer on the CalMS21 test set. We can see the model does best on the dominant Other class, while Attack is the hardest class. This reflects the severe class imbalance in the dataset.

Figure 5. Structure of the multi-scale attention module. The modulated input Ẑ0 is passed through three parallel branches: short-range causal attention on the first ⌊T/2⌋ frames (this branch focuses on rapid motion cues occurring within short temporal contexts), medium-range causal attention on all T frames, and global bidirectional attention on all T +1 tokens. The short-range and medium-range outputs are averaged on overlapping positions to produce Alocal , which is combined with Aglobal through residual addition and layer normalization.
Table 4. Frame distribution of behavior classes across training and test splits of the CalMS21 Task 1 dataset (total 769,845 frames across 89 videos).
Table 5. Comparison of the two evaluation datasets.

Figure 11. Boundary error analysis results on the CalMS21 dataset. The left plot illustrates the variation in prediction accuracy with respect to the distance from the nearest behavior transition, while the right plot presents the distribution of samples observed at each distance.
Table 11. Ablation study results on the Valid-8–Test-2 split of the RatSI dataset.
Figure 8. Confusion matrices obtained for the Valid-2–Test-8, Valid-3–Test-9, and Valid-7–Test-3 splits.
